coredata.dc:title: Designed controllable nitrogen-doped carbon-dots-loaded MoP nanoparticles for boosting hydrogen evolution reaction in alkaline medium
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Haoqiang
Li, Yuhang
Shang, Lu
Tang, Zhiyong
Zhang, Tierui
Lu, Siyu
coredata.dc:description: The development of inexpensive, highly active, and stable non-precious metal catalysts is the key to efficient H2 production by the electrolysis of water. Based on initial theoretical predictions, we report the design and synthesis of N-doped, defect-containing, carbon-dots (CDs)-loaded molybdenum phosphide (MoP/CDs) nanoparticles using CDs with different N contents. This optimized composite material performed outstandingly in the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), with an overpotential of 70 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 with a 1.0 M KOH electrolyte. N doping changed the electronic arrangement around the reaction site, promoting the adsorption of HER intermediates. Increasing the N content further activated the adjacent C atoms to detach due to the more negative C vacancy formation energies resulting in a defect site with lost atoms and changed bond lengths. The introduction of defects increases the number of dangling bonds adjacent to reaction sites and reduces site coordination numbers, changing their electrocatalytic activity. Results show that N-doped and defect-containing MoP/CDs nanoparticles have excellent H2 production performance during the electrolysis of alkaline solution. These findings open a new area of application of heteroatom-doped CDs and provide new ideas for the design of efficient carbon-semiconductor composite HER catalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nitrogen-doped
MoP
Hydrogen evolution reaction

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Beijing Municipality
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The variation of carbon vacancy formation energy with the nitrogen content; The different formation energies for the given nitrogen content stand for the varied defect site locations, corresponding to the configurations shown in Fig. S1; (b) Partial density of states of the molybdenum atom interacted with the defect site on carbon; Dotted line is Fermi level; (c) Energy variations for the hydrogen evolution reaction proceeding on C–MoP, 1NC-MoP, 2NC-MoP and 3NC-MoP structures; The inset figure illustrates that the theoretical overpotential is reduced as the hydrogen adsorption energy decreases; (d) Local configurations for the corresponding adsorbed hydrogen intermediates.

TEM images of CA-CDs (a), CE-CDs (b), CM-CDs (c); UV–Vis absorption, PL excitation and PL emission spectra of the CA-CDs (d), CE-CDs (e), and CM-CDs (f); insets show photographs of CDs in aqueous solution under visible (left) and UV (right) light, respectively; XPS survey spectra (g); XRD patterns (h); FT-IR spectra (i) of the CA-CDs, CE-CDs and CM-CDs.

(a) XRD pattern of MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100; (b) Raman spectra of MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100, MoP/0.5CE-CDs1100 and MoP/CA-CDs1100; (c–e) TEM images of MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 under different magnifications; (f–k) HAADF image and EDX elemental mapping images of Mo, P, C, N, and Overlap in MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100.
XPS spectra for XPS full spectrum (a), C 1s (b), N 1s (c), Mo 3d (d) and P 2p (e) in MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100; (f) XRD pattern of MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 catalyst after the long-term stability test.

(a) Polarization curves and (b) corresponding Tafel slopes of MoO2/NO-CDs, MoP/0.5CA-CDs1100, MoP/0.5CE-CDs1100, MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 and Pt/C at pH = 14 with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1; (c) Nyquist curves for MoO2/NO-CDs, MoP/0.5CA-CDs1100, MoP/0.5CE-CDs1100, MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 and Pt/C catalyst; (d) Durability test for MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 in 1.0 M KOH before and after 1000 cycles. Inset: The current-time (i–t) curves of MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 at a constant overpotential for 12 h; (e) Polarization curves for the MoP/CM-CDs annealed at different temperature and Pt/C catalyst in 1 M KOH electrolyte with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1; (f) Polarization curves of MoP/CM-CDs1100 prepared by adding different CM-CDs ratios and commercial Pt/C in 1.0 M KOH electrolyte with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic route employed for preparation of the N-doped and defect-induced MoP/CDs catalyst.

head.ce:title: Designed controllable nitrogen-doped carbon-dots-loaded MoP nanoparticles for boosting hydrogen evolution reaction in alkaline medium

Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Haoqiang Song and Yuhang Li contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of inexpensive, highly active, and stable non-precious metal catalysts is the key to efficient H2 production by the electrolysis of water. Based on initial theoretical predictions, we report the design and synthesis of N-doped, defect-containing, carbon-dots (CDs)-loaded molybdenum phosphide (MoP/CDs) nanoparticles using CDs with different N contents. This optimized composite material performed outstandingly in the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), with an overpotential of 70 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 with a 1.0 M KOH electrolyte. N doping changed the electronic arrangement around the reaction site, promoting the adsorption of HER intermediates. Increasing the N content further activated the adjacent C atoms to detach due to the more negative C vacancy formation energies resulting in a defect site with lost atoms and changed bond lengths. The introduction of defects increases the number of dangling bonds adjacent to reaction sites and reduces site coordination numbers, changing their electrocatalytic activity. Results show that N-doped and defect-containing MoP/CDs nanoparticles have excellent H2 production performance during the electrolysis of alkaline solution. These findings open a new area of application of heteroatom-doped CDs and provide new ideas for the design of efficient carbon-semiconductor composite HER catalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen is a renewable, clean energy carrier and a future candidate for being an ideal green energy source [1–5]. Various sources of hydrogen energy have been considered, particular attention has been paid to hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) due to their resultant high H2 purity, high energy conversion efficiency, and low environmental impact [6–8]. Under acidic conditions, the electrolysis of water is not suitable for industrial H2 production, but water molecules are more difficult to activate under alkaline conditions, making electrocatalytic H2 production challenging [9,10]. Alkaline HER includes two main steps: initial electron-coupling hydrolysis and subsequent hydrogen adsorption (and thus H2 gas formation) [11]. The most active Pt-based catalysts cannot currently be used on a large scale due to their scarcity and high cost, and HER commercialization therefore critically depends on the development of high-efficiency, non-precious-metal-based catalysts.

In recent years, Mo-based materials such as Mo2C [12], MoS2 [7,13,14], MoO2 [15,16], MoSe2 [17], and MoP [18–22] have been investigated for their high catalytic activity and low price. MoP has received particular attention due to its high electrical conductivity in electrolyzed H2 production. The carrier of the HER catalyst significantly affects its activity, and carbon nanomaterials (e.g., graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, and carbon black) have been studied as HER catalyst carriers because of their high specific surface area, high electrical conductivity, acid and alkali corrosion resistance, and environmental friendliness [23]. However, these carbon materials have drawbacks including high raw-material costs, and complicated processing. As a zero-dimensional carbon material, carbon dots (CDs) are ideal materials for use as conductive carbon carriers because of their simple synthesis, low cost, electrical conductivity, high stability, non-polluting production, and easy surface functionalization [24]. Their surfaces can host many functional groups (e.g., –NH2, –OH, and –COOH) that are able to coordinate with metal ions or semiconductors with empty d orbitals to form relatively stable CDs-metal ion or CDs-semiconductor coordinated composites [9]. Such composites can be converted to loaded catalysts by controlled heat treatment. During this transformation, metal nanoparticles are confined between CDs, effectively preventing them from growing in the catalytic composite [25]. Further control of material properties is possible by simply selecting appropriate precursor molecules for CDs synthesis; providing simultaneous control of CDs doping with different heteroatoms, and thus also the electronic arrangement around the reaction site, which can be tailored to suit the adsorption of HER intermediates [26]. Controlling the reaction in this way introduces defects that increase the number of dangling bonds adjacent to the reaction site, reducing the coordination number of reaction sites and adjusting site electrocatalytic activity [27]. Composites of MoP and CDs for use as effective electrolyzed H2 catalysts are in an early stage of development. However, their potentially ideal properties indicate the necessity of studying combinations of MoP and CDs with different N contents for use in HER electrocatalysis.

Activity towards HER is strongly correlated with the Gibbs free energy of H2 adsorption (ΔGH*) on the catalyst surface, with strong HER electrocatalysis involving material with a ΔGH* value close to 0 eV [28,29]. This is because a lower ΔGH* slows the hydrogen-release step due to H bonding being too strong, whereas a higher ΔGH* makes proton- and electron-transfer steps endothermic [30]. Carbon vacancy defects were investigated in this work using density functional theory (DFT), as shown in Fig. 1 a. C vacancy formation in a perfect graphene matrix was taken as a reference. As each C atom is replaced by N, the relative C vacancy formation energies become more negative, suggesting that C atoms detach progressively more easily, especially when replaced by bonding to N (−2.99 eV). As the N content increases, C atoms adjacent to N are further activated to detach due to the more negative C vacancy-formation energies, resulting in a defect site with lost C atoms and changed bond lengths. Overall, N doping can effectively induce C defect formation. Cases with the most negative formation energies were adopted for the following investigations. The interfacial structure involving C and MoP was characterized by an analysis of partial density of states (PDOS) of the Mo atoms located at the C vacancy sites, as shown in Fig. 1b. Comparing 2NC-MoP with 1NC-MoP shows that electron tends to occupy a higher energy level (closer to the Fermi level) when the N content increases. For 3NC-MoP, the PDOS peak does not shift positively but increases, similarly indicating increased electron density near the Fermi level. This tendency suggests that the interaction between defect sites and Mo atoms can efficiently enhance charge transfer at the C–MoP interface. Fig. 1c shows the calculated HER energy evolutions. All the structures with MoP exhibited strong hydrogen adsorption, and consequently a large HER overpotential. Compared with a C–MoP-only structure, incorporation of N and defect sites appears to reduce the H2 adsorption energy, with greater reductions achieved by increasing N content. The inset in Fig. 1c clearly illustrates the volcano correlation between the HER theoretical overpotential and H2 adsorption energy, where the increasing N content contributes to climbing the volcano.

Given these promising DFT predictions, we used CDs with different N contents as C and N sources, phytic acid (PA) as a P source, and ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate (H32Mo7N6O28) as a Mo source. Three CDs-loaded Mo-based composites with different N doping amounts were successfully synthesized using CDs precursors with different N contents (no N source, 2 N source, and 6 N source) in a novel self-assembly strategy (2 N means that one molecule that provides the N source has two nitrogen atoms in it, the same with 6 N), with subsequent pyrolysis under an Ar atmosphere. Defects were introduced by controlling reaction conditions. Note that other synthetic N-doped and defect-containing nanoparticle hybrids can also be used in this way. Prepared CDs-loaded Mo phosphide (denoted as MoP/YCDsX, X represents the temperature of calcination of the precursor, Y represents the number of CDs added to the precursor) nanoparticle materials containing N-doping and induced defects are capable of high H2 production during water electrolysis, and have thus been researched for H2 production by water electrolysis. Our study found that the optimal nanomaterial has a corresponding overpotential of 70 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 in a 1.0 M KOH solution, with a Tafel slope of 77.49 mV dec−1. The novel N-doped C structure imparts excellent electrochemical properties to the MoP/CDs complex.

Scheme 1 depicts the synthesis of the catalysts. Specifically, CDs prepared using citric acid (CA; denoted as CA-CDs), citric acid and ethylenediamine (CA and EDA; denoted as CE-CDs), citric acid and melamine (CA and MA; denoted as CM-CDs) as raw materials were mixed with a Mo compound and PA, and then pyrolyzed to obtain the final catalysts.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 2 a–c) of CA-CDs, CE-CDs, and CM-CDs show them to be well dispersed, with diameters of approximately 2.64 nm, 1.63 nm, and 4.05 nm (Fig. S2; Supporting Information), respectively. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CA-CDs (Fig. 2d) shows no apparent maximum absorption wavelength. Absorption bands at 238 nm and 340 nm appear in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CE-CDs (Fig. 2e), and are assigned to the π→π* transition of the conjugated olefin, and n→π* transitions of a class of unsaturated double bonds containing heteroatoms, such as CO and NO, respectively [31]. The maximum absorption wavelengths at 236 nm and 331 nm observed in the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CM-CDs (Fig. 2f) are also respectively attributed to π→π* transitions of conjugated olefins and the n→π* transition of a class of unsaturated double bonds containing heteroatoms, such as CO and NO. When excited by 336 nm light, CA-CDs showed blue luminescence centered at 431 nm (Fig. 2d), while excitation at 348 nm caused blue luminescence in CE-CDs at 442 nm (Fig. 2e). Stronger blue luminescence at 466 nm was stimulated in CM-CDs by 376 nm light (Fig. 2f). These results indicate that the three products had different surface states that affect their CDs band gaps [32]. X-ray photoelectron energy spectroscopy (XPS) was used to study elemental contents of the three sets of CDs. The survey spectrum (Fig. 2g) revealed C and O in CA-CDs and C, N, and O in CE-CDs and CM-CDs, with CM-CDs having a higher N content than CE-CDs (Table S1, Supporting Information). These data indicate both the successful incorporation of N into the CDs and control of its doping.

The structures of the three CDs products were investigated by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD). Fig. 2h shows a broad peak for CE-CDs and CM-CDs at 24.43° and 21.00°, respectively. These two broad peaks correspond to (002) planes of carbon, and indicate the formation of amorphous carbon. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. 2i) show stretching vibrations of O–H (3444.4 cm−1), CC (1652.8 cm−1), NO (1558.0 cm−1), and C–N (1128.3 cm−1) functional groups and bending vibrations of C–H (1385.8 cm−1) in CE-CDs. CM-CDs showed telescopic vibrations of O–H (3448.1 cm−1), N–H (3122.2 cm−1), CO (1706.7 cm−1), and CC (1625.7 cm−1) functional groups and bending vibrations of C–H (1396.2 cm−1) [33]. CA-CDs also showed multiple vibration absorption peaks. Overall, the FTIR data indicate that the three CDs contained multiple functional groups, with CE-CDs and CM-CDs showing N-containing groups.

The electrocatalysts were next examined by XRD. Fig. S3 (Supporting Information) indicates that adding no CDs to the precursor led to oxides of Mo forming in the product, as demonstrated by the precursor containing more O than otherwise. Precursors containing CA-CDs, CE-CDs, or CM-CDs led to MoP forming after pyrolysis (Fig. S4 and Fig. S5, Supporting Information; Fig. 3 a). The resulting XRD patterns correspond to the hexagonal structure of MoP (Fig. 3a; JCPDS No. 89–5110; a = b = 3.23 Å, and c = 3.21 Å), indicating that the (CA-CDs)-PA-Mo, (CE-CDs)-PA-Mo, and (CM-CDs)-PA-Mo precursors were transformed into MoP nanocrystals during annealing. These results show that CDs can promote the conversion of molybdenum oxide to molybdenum phosphide. Fig. 3b shows clear D- and G-bands at approximately 1338 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1, respectively. The calculated I
D
/I
G intensity ratio of MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 of 1.13 reveals an abundance of defects and disorders in the graphene sheets. The lower intensity ratios for MoP/0.5CE-CDs1100 and MoP/0.5CA-CDs1100 (1.08 and 1.00, respectively) are attributed to the precursor of MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 containing more N atoms, which escaped at high temperature [29]. The morphology of the electrocatalyst was then investigated by TEM. The high-resolution TEM image of MoP/0.5CE-CDs1100 in Fig. S6 (Supporting Information) clearly shows two distinct phases (Fig. S6b; Supporting Information). The typical lattice distance of 0.21 nm corresponds to the (101) plane of the hexagonal structure of MoP, while the inter-planar spacing of 0.28 nm is assigned to the (100) plane of MoP. Fig. 3c–e presents TEM images at different scales of MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 showing the interconnected carbon layers, which are electrically conductive and beneficial for decreasing the internal resistance of the hybrid material. During the pyrolysis process, CDs were self-crosslinking to form interconnected carbon layer because of hydrogen bonding between the surface functional groups of the CDs (e.g., amino (-NH2), carboxyl (-COOH), and hydroxyl (-OH) groups) and Van Der Waals bonding induced between adjacent CDs [9]. Elemental mappings were also conducted to survey the elemental distributions of C, N, P, and Mo in MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 nanoparticles (Fig. 3g–k). Signals for all four elements appear uniformly spread over the nanosheets, indicating their homogeneous distribution. Inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy determined the MoP content of MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 to be 17.9 wt%. These results confirm that the sample was N-doped and contained defect-induced MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 nanoparticles.

Characterization by XPS determined the surface composition and chemical state of each element in MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 (Fig. 4 a–e). The survey spectrum confirmed the presence of C, N, O, Mo, and P (Fig. 4a). The C 1s peak was fitted as three different signals (Fig. 4b), whose binding energies of 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV, and 288.9 eV are ascribed to C–C, C–N, and O–CO, respectively. The high-resolution Mo 3d XPS spectrum shows two peaks located at binding energies of 228.4 eV and 231.6 eV, corresponding to Mo 3d 5/2 and Mo 3d 3/2 of Moδ+ species (0 < δ < 4) (Fig. 4d), respectively, which are typical peaks for Mo in MoP. Two other doublets at 236.1/233.3 eV (Mo6+ 3d 3/2/Mo6+ 3d 5/2) and 232.5/229.8 eV (Mo4+ 3d 3/2/Mo4+ 3d 5/2) are attributable to high oxidation states of Mo (MoO3 and MoO2) due to the surface oxidation of MoP [18,34,35]. Similarly, three doublets with binding energies of 228.4 eV and 231.6 eV for Moδ+, 233.3 eV and 236.1 eV for Mo6+, and 229.8 eV and 232.5 eV for Mo4+ can be assigned to MoP, MoO3, and MoO2, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of P 2p is fitted to two peaks with binding energies of 130.7 eV and 129.9 eV, which are ascribed to MoP. Note that P is negatively charged (Pδ–) here, as indicated by its lower binding energy than that of P0 (130.1 eV) [36]. The N 1s XPS spectrum of MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 indicates the presence of pyridinic N (399.0 eV), pyrrolic N (400.2 eV), and graphitic N (401.5 eV) (Fig. 4c) [20,36,37]. The peak at 395.3 eV was assigned to Mo 3p, and no peak belonging to Mo–N was observed, indicating that N tended to doped into CM-CDs rather than MoP. The N atoms thus doped into CM-CDs in MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100. The content of N in MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 was determined to be 6.07 at.% (Table S2).

We also analyzed the surface compositions of MoP/0.5CE-CDs1100 and MoP/0.5CA-CDs1100 (Fig. S7 and Fig. S8; Supporting Information). XPS data showed MoP/0.5CE-CDs1100 to comprise mainly C, N, O, Mo, and P. Its N 1s XPS spectrum revealed pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, indicating that N also successfully doped into CE-CDs. The 3.41 at.% N content is less than that in MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100, which is consistent with the CE-CDs having fewer N atoms than CM-CDs. The XPS data identified C, O, Mo, and P as the main elements in MoP/0.5CA-CDs1100, with N being absent. The presence of N dopants can change the electron density of CDs, promoting HER activity via the cooperative effects of MoP and N-doped CDs. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface areas of the prepared samples (Fig. S9; Supporting Information) were obtained by recording N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77 K. MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 had a higher BET specific surface area of 755.5 m2 g−1. The corresponding BJH pore-size distribution curve showed its average pore diameter to be 2.8 nm. This indicates that high temperature caused some substances in the precursor to pyrolyze to form a mesoporous structure. The resulting porous structure has more active sites accessible to promote H2 production during electrolysis. After a series of electrochemical tests, the materials were observed by XRD, XPS, and TEM (Fig. 4f and Figs. S10–S12; Supporting Information). The results show that the surface chemical state and morphology of MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 remain unchanged after electrochemical testing. Note that the peaks of F in the XPS survey spectrum are for residues from the Nafion binder.

In order to investigate the effect of CDs N content on catalyst electrochemical properties, catalysts were prepared under conditions of no CDs, CA-CDs, CE-CDs, and CM-CDs (labelled as MoO2/NO-CDs, MoP/0.5CA-CDs1100, MoP/0.5CE-CDs1100, and MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100, respectively) while keeping all other conditions constant. The HER electrocatalytic activities of the MoP/CDs electrocatalysts were then determined using a standard three-electrode system in aqueous 1.0 M KOH. All potentials here refer to reversible hydrogen potential. For comparison, the catalytic activities of commercial Pt/C (20 wt%) were also tested under the same conditions. Fig. 5 a shows that when no CDs were added, the corresponding overpotential at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 was 614 mV. Using CA-CDs with no N led to poor electrocatalytic performance due to the lack of N doping. N-containing CE-CDs improved the performance of the catalyst. The corresponding overpotential at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 was 120 mV. Increasing the N content of the CDs further improved the catalytic activity, and the overpotential of MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 at 10 mA cm−2 was 70 mV, similar to the commercial Pt/C catalyst (32 mV). These results show that the important role of N doped induced carbon defects in HER by MoP/CDs composite in alkaline solution. More notably, MoP/0.5CE-CDs1100 outperformed commercial Pt/C at a high current density (Fig. S13; Supporting Information). The Tafel slope calculated by polarization curves and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Fig. 5b and c) have the same regularity as Fig. 5a, showing that the high-N-content MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 catalyst had the best HER performance. The Tafel slope of MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 (77.49 mV dec−1, Fig. 5b) was slightly higher than that of Pt/C (54.45 mV dec−1), but lower than those of MoP/0.5CE-CDs1100 (98.61 mV dec−1) and MoP/0.5CA-CDs1100 (102.57 mV dec−1), indicating the Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism in 1.0 M KOH [38,39]. Moreover, the EIS results (Fig. 5c) show that MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 had a smaller charge transfer resistance than either MoP/0.5CE-CDs1100 or MoP/0.5CA-CDs1100. The insert in Fig. 5c gives the model equivalent circuit used for data fitting of sample EIS spectra. The stability of the MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 catalyst was subsequently demonstrated for 1000 cyclic voltammetry cycles (Fig. 5d), during which its overpotential changed little at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The accompanying insert gives an i-t curve scanned for 12 h at constant voltage that also demonstrates the material's stability. All these results confirm the efficient catalytic activity and excellent stability of MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 nanoparticles.

To explore their formation mechanism and intrinsic electrocatalytic activity, MoP/0.5CM-CDs samples were prepared with annealing at 800 °C, 900 °C, 1000 °C, and 1100 °C (labelled from MoP/0.5CM-CDs800 to MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100) while keeping all other conditions constant. XRD analysis showed that when the temperature initially rose to 900 °C MoP formed, with further heating enhancing its crystallinity (Fig. S14; Supporting Information). FTIR spectroscopy identified that the electrocatalyst surfaces obtained at different temperatures contained different functional groups (Fig. S15; Supporting Information). Raman spectroscopy found that the number of C defects in the CDs increased with calcination temperature, which was attributed to the increasing escape of doped N atoms in the CDs at higher temperatures (Fig. S16; Supporting Information). Increases in the number of C defects increases the number of edge sites in the catalyst, increasing the amount of dangling bonds in the CDs, which facilitate the anchoring of MoP, and thereby increases the stability of the material. The electrocatalytic HER activities of MoP/0.5CM-CDs800 to MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 were determined using a standard three-electrode setup in aqueous 1.0 M KOH (Fig. 5e). Calcination at 1100 °C led to the highest HER activity among the four tested catalysts. Their overpotentials were ranked MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 (70 mV) < MoP/0.5CM-CDs1000 (93 mV) < MoP/0.5CM-CDs900 (134 mV) < MoP/0.5CM-CDs800 (296 mV) (Fig. 5e), indicating that activity increased with annealing temperature. Tafel plots further elucidated the HER kinetics of the electrocatalysts. Fig. S17 (Supporting Information) shows that Pt/C exhibited the lowest Tafel slope. However, the Tafel slope of MoP/0.5CM-CDs1100 (77.49 mV dec−1) indicates that its HER kinetics followed the Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism. The Tafel slope for the MoP/0.5CM-CDs samples prepared with annealing at 1100 °C was much lower than those of the control samples, and implies more favourable kinetics and higher catalytic activity toward the HER. EIS results for MoP/CM-CDs800, MoP/CM-CDs900, MoP/CM-CDs1000, MoP/CM-CDs1100, and commercial Pt/C catalyst (20 wt%) (Fig. S18; Supporting Information) reveal that MoP/CM-CDs1100 contain more active sites than catalysts calcined at other temperatures.

To explore the optimum proportion of MoP to CM-CDs in an HER catalyst, a benchmark of 1.26 g CM-CDs was used while keeping the other reaction conditions unchanged. Multiples (0.25, 0.5, 0.75, and 1.5) of the benchmark mass of CM-CDs were then reacted, and the obtained samples were subjected to XRD and Raman analysis (Fig. S19 and Fig. S20; Supporting Information). The resulting data indicate the generation of MoP in the samples. When CM-CDs were used at 0.5 times the benchmark mass, defects in the CDs were the most abundant. H2 production during water electrolysis in an alkaline solution was measured (Fig. 5f and Fig. S21; Supporting Information). Using CM-CDs at half the reference mass led to the optimal (i.e., smallest) overpotential and Tafel slope (70 mV and 77.49 mV dec−1, respectively) among the considered catalysts with different masses of CM-CDs. Fig. S22 shows that this sample had the lowest impedance in the test group. The above data demonstrate that the optimal catalyst requires an optimal ratio of CDs and MoP.

DFT calculations were used in designing a new, controllable, environmentally friendly method for producing N-doped CDs for the preparation of N-doped, defect-containing, CDs-loaded MoP nanoparticles by preparing and converting Mo-PA-CDs. The DFT calculation results demonstrated that the N and defect sites can reduce the H2 adsorption energy, which could be further lowered by increasing the N content. By designing and regulating the N-doping content in the electrocatalyst carbon carrier and by controlling the calcination temperature and the proportion of CDs participating in the reaction, electron arrangements surrounding the reaction sites were altered in such a way as to promote the adsorption of HER intermediates. The electron transfer rate between the carrier and the active site was therefore accelerated, and the production of H2 during electrolysis of water improved. In 1.0 M KOH, the optimized composite nanomaterial had an overpotential of 70 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. Our work has opened a broad area of research for the design and manufacture of heteroatom-doped, CDs-loaded, transition-metal phosphide nanoparticles, providing a new way of studying various electrocatalytic materials.

Haoqiang Song: Writing - original draft, Formal analysis. Yuhang Li: Methodology, Software, Writing - original draft, Investigation. Lu Shang: Writing - review & editing, Formal analysis. Zhiyong Tang: Writing - review & editing. Tierui Zhang: Writing - review & editing, Validation, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Siyu Lu: Writing - review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21905253, 51973200, 51825205, 21871279), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M640681, 2019T120632) and the Beijing Natural Science Foundation (2191002, 2182078).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Haoqiang Song was born in 1994 and is currently conducting his master's research at College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. His main research focuses on the synthesis of carbon-based materials and their electrocatalytic applications.

Dr. Yuhang Li is currently a postdoctoral researcher, cooperated with Prof. Yexiang Tong as supervisor, in School of Chemistry at Sun Yat-sen University, China. He received his B.S. degree (2014) in Applied Chemistry and Ph.D. degree (2018) in Industrial Catalysis from South China University of Technology, under the supervision of Prof. Feng Peng. His researches are focused on the first principles calculations toward electrochemical reaction mechanism in energy conversion applications.

Dr. Lu Shang is currently an associate professor at the Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. He received his Ph.D. degree in physical chemistry from the Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2013. His present research focuses on the design of nanocatalysts and their applications for energy storage/conversion systems, such as fuel cell and CO2 electroreduction.

Prof. Tierui Zhang is a full professor at the Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. He obtained his Ph.D. degree in Chemistry in 2003 at Jilin University, China. He then worked as a post-doctoral researcher in the labs of Prof. Markus Antonietti, Prof. Charl F.J. Faul, Prof. Hicham Fenniri, Prof. Z. RyanTian, Prof. Yadong Yin, and Prof. Yushan Yan. His current scientific interests focus on catalyst nanomaterials for energy conversion.

Dr. Siyu Lu is currently an associate professor of College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. He received his Ph.D. degree in polymer physics and chemistry under the supervision of Prof. Bai Yang at Jilin University. His research interests relate to the design, preparation, properties of photoelectric nanocrystals (Carbon dots, Semiconductors, etc.) and their applications in Catalysis, Fluorescence and DFT calculation.
